text: We report here a standardized technique of the realization of a neovessi robotized intracorporeal on the Studer technique, taking a distance of fifty-five centimeters from the distal lobe, with an afferent axis of twenty centimeters to the retro-ideal anastomosis, an afferent axis of forty centimeters in length, including a chimney of fifteen centimeters.
The surgeon performs the retro-ideal anastomosis at a point twenty centimeters from the distal extremity, prior to the tubularization process.
The surgeon insulates the lens to maintain an appropriate distance between the distal extremity and the cecum, facilitating the creation of an ileo-ileal anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an ileal section using a GIA stapler, cutting through the ileum to prepare for further anastomosis.
The surgeon prepares the ileal segment to create a chimney for the uretero-ileal anastomoses, ensuring proper alignment and positioning for the upcoming connection.
The surgeon restores digestive continuity by performing a lateral-lateral ileo-ileal anastomosis using an endo-GA-XX clip, followed by an endo-GA-XXXV clip to extend the length of the anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the anastomosis using an endo-GA-XX clip, ensuring all fragments with questionable vitality are secured within the closure.
The surgeon creates the urethral neo on the most mobile portion of the tissue towards the urethra, preparing for the uretero-ileal anastomosis.
The surgeon performs the urethral anastomosis to stabilize the neovesicle onto the urethra, ensuring secure attachment and continuity.
The surgical field reveals the two legs of the neovessi, which are exposed for further manipulation or anastomosis.

To avoid the traction on the anastomosis in order to avoid the rupture of the urethra or the intestinal gills, it is possible to reduce the position between an endobrachium at fifteen degrees by redocking the robot, reduce the importance of the peritoneum, perform perineal pushes or detubularize the intestinal gills before performing the anastomosis.
It is also possible to put in place two acts from one end to the other of the gluteus maximus to lower the gluteus maximus on the rectus.
The proximal extremity
The surgeon positions the chimney in contact with the ureters, preparing for a future anastomosis.
The surgeon begins the detubularization of the lancet and hall, starting from the most distal portion on the right side and progressing towards the urethra.
A fragment of intestinal tissue, approximately one centimeter in width, is left in place on the urethral anastomosis to achieve the ideal connection.
The surgeon reassembles the detubulated segment, shaping it into a ferrous lance configuration, extending towards the anus.
The surgeon initiates the posterior suture, focusing on the area near the previously positioned ureters for anastomosis.
The surgeon uses Quiltra sutures in a three-zero pattern, starting from the bottom and gradually moving upwards to form a posterior plate.
The surgeon prepares to perform the urethral anastomosis, ensuring the alignment of the urethra with the previously prepared intestinal segment.
The surgeon performs the ideal urethral anastomosis using Wallace's technique, with visible suturing of the urethra to the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon fixes the ureters to the abdominal wall, enhancing exposure for the anastomosis procedure.
The surgeon opens the ureters laterally and performs a uretero-ureteral anastomosis using a continuous suture with PDS 5-0, ensuring precise alignment of the ureteral ends.
The surgeon continues the suture from the bottom upwards, progressively forming a posterior plate until reaching a higher stop.
Urethral probes are inserted into the anastomoses, guided by terrumen guides, to ensure proper intubation.
In this example, we have chosen monogy probes of CH-VIII caliber.
The surgeon systematically fixes the probes in place to prevent any untimely mobilization during the procedure.
The surgeon exteriorizes the extremities of the hematoma through the neuvesis, followed by performing the uretero-ileal anastomosis using two surges.
The surgeon performs the first suture on the anterior face, restoring continuity between the ureter and ileum.

to the many stages of intra-corporal robotic neovestia.
The surgeon performs the plication of the neovesica, shaping it into a circular form to ensure good compliance.
The surgeon lowers the lateral right extremity of the neovessi to the left side, positioning it near the site of the retro-ileal anastomosis.
The surgeon performs two sutures on the neovessi, with one suture placed horizontally to the right side of the neovessi.
The surgeon gradually moves upwards towards the upper right end of the surgical field, adjusting the position of the neovessi.
The surgeon performs several continuous sutures using two quills and 3-0 suture material, adjusting the length to accommodate the surgical requirements.
The surgeon performs the second surge, starting from the lower stop point and gradually advancing towards the left edge of the neovessi, manipulating the tissue to ensure proper alignment.
The surgeon completes the closure of the neovessi and performs a waterproof test to ensure the integrity of the closure.
The fully robotized intracorporeal neovessi technique is a totally mini-invasive alternative to the reconstruction of a neovessi.
It requires more learning related to the sequence of times and requires a standardization rather than the complexity of the gestures performed.
